source_texts.title: Initial Stakeholder Interview on ASPERA-3 Data System Requirements
source_texts.text:
Alice [Project Manager]: Thanks everyone for joining. Let's start with the overall scope. We need to ensure the system handles all data processing and archiving for the Mars Express mission, specifically for ASPERA-3. Bob, can you elaborate on telemetry acquisition?

Bob [Data Engineer]: Sure, Alice. We have to pull telemetry data from both NISN and ESOC. That includes not just the instrument data but also spacecraft orbit and attitude telemetry. It's critical for the analysis.

Clara [Science Analyst]: Right, and once we get that raw telemetry, the system should convert it into those Instrument Data File Sets, or IDFS. These sets need to include all science data plus any engineering or ancillary info needed for calibration and validation.

Alice: So, the IDFS should be organized logically?

Bob: Exactly. We divide the physical components of ASPERA-3 into virtual instruments, each represented as its own IDFS data set. This helps the science team focus on specific parts.

David [Archive Specialist]: What if ESOC doesn't provide cleaned-up telemetry?

Bob: Then the system has to generate intermediate cleaned-up files ourselves for both ASPERA-3 and orbit/attitude data.

Clara: And all these files—raw telemetry, IDFS, intermediate cleaned files—need to be stored locally at SwRI's archive.

David: Plus, after calibration and validation, the IDFS data sets must be submitted to the Planetary Data System within six months.

Alice: That timeline is tight. How about data distribution?

Clara: The system should distribute all relevant data and intermediate files electronically to all Co-Investigators within 24 hours of telemetry acquisition, assuming no errors in transmission or processing.

Oh wait, before we move on, For the first rollout, we'd like APAF to skip any local error handling and just pass exceptions straight up to the orchestrator unchanged. No internal retries, no circuit breakers, and please don't wrap or mask errors—the gateway's global handler will take care of all that. This keeps failure behavior consistent across services.

Bob: Also, we want web-based displays for the latest data, but access should be password protected until the data is publicly released.

Actually, one more thing I wanted to bring up — From our side, the verification demos have to be executed on the lab’s HIL bench using the proprietary DAQ and the calibrated vibration table. We won’t accept a field demonstration without that special equipment, because QA needs the bench traces for sign-off.

David: The ASPERA-3 team defines these displays, right?

Clara: Yes, and the web server itself should have password protection where needed to restrict access to authorized team members.

Alice: What about system reliability?

Bob: We'll build in error handling mechanisms to ensure the system stays dependable.

David: On the hardware side, we need UNIX-based development and production machines with archival capabilities, plus a web server.

Alice: Network connectivity?

Bob: There must be a reliable network link between SwRI and NISN for data transfers.

Clara: We also need to provide Co-Investigators with access to IDFS data and the analysis software developed by SwRI.

Alice: Can we use the www.ASPERA-3.org site for data review?

Bob: That's the plan.

Sorry, going back to something I mentioned earlier — Before we get into features, I want to stress safety. The system must not introduce any hazards to our staff, to facilities or equipment, or to the environment. If something could put people at risk, damage property, or harm the environment, we need to rethink it.

David: Regarding documentation, the APAF Operations Procedures Document should clearly define distribution mechanisms, installation-dependent requirements, and provide detailed instructions so users don't need extra training.

Alice: And the Software Design Document?

Bob: It must fully describe all data products within each virtual instrument, including parameters and internal interfaces.

Clara: Also, every requirement should be clear, testable, and feasible within schedule and budget.

David: We need unique IDs for each requirement, annotated with verification methods and traceability.

Alice: Verification methods should include demonstrations without special equipment and analysis using calibration data.

Bob: We can simulate instrument data sets from calibration data, process them into IDFS, and analyze outputs with the Southwest Data Display and Analysis System.

Clara: The entire integrated ASPERA-3 data set must be provided to Co-Investigators and archived in the NASA PDS.

David: Don't forget the energy range of measurements—0.1 to 100 keV.

Alice: SwRI is responsible for developing the data reduction system and coordinating with sensor teams on data packetization.

Bob: The processing software must meet IRF delivery requirements to support the Mars Express launch and operations schedule.

Clara: SwRI will also provide processing algorithms to IRF on an agreed timeline.

David: The MO PI will collect engineering and ancillary info needed to validate and calibrate scientific data before PDS deposit.

Alice: Validation and calibration time should be minimized but must not exceed six months after acquisition.

Clara: We want to integrate science analysis software developed by the MO PI team into the NASA-approved data repository, providing equal online access to the PI team and the wider science community.

David: The project data management plan must cover planning, acquisition, processing, validation, archiving, and preservation, defining roles and interfaces.

Alice: System operation should be restricted to a single mode for simplicity.

Bob: And safety is paramount—the system can't pose hazards to personnel, property, or environment.

Clara: That covers most points. Any questions?

Alice: None from me. Thanks all for the input.

source_texts.title: ASPERA-3 Data System Design Kickoff Meeting
source_texts.text:
Date: March 15, 2025
Attendees: Emily (Project Lead), Frank (Software Architect), Grace (Security Officer), Helen (Operations Manager), Ian (Data Scientist)

Purpose: To align on initial design considerations and operational constraints for the ASPERA-3 Processing and Archiving Facility (APAF) data system.

Discussion:

- Emily opened with the need to ensure the system processes and archives Mars Express mission data effectively, emphasizing compliance with NASA PDS standards.

- Frank outlined the system architecture, confirming the use of UNIX-based development and production environments. He stressed the importance of including an archival system integrated with the production environment.

- Helen highlighted operational constraints, noting the system must operate in only one mode to simplify control and reduce complexity.

- Grace raised concerns about data security, recommending password protection on all web-based data displays and servers to restrict access to authorized ASPERA-3 team members until public release.

- Ian discussed data acquisition, confirming telemetry will be received via NISN and ESOC, including spacecraft orbit and attitude data. He stressed the necessity of generating intermediate cleaned-up telemetry files if ESOC does not provide them.

- Emily emphasized the requirement to distribute data and any intermediate files electronically to all Co-Investigators within 24 hours of telemetry acquisition, assuming error-free transmission and processing.

- Frank confirmed the system will provide web-based displays of current data, with access control as per Grace's security recommendations.

- Follow-up point: Quick check on APAF: we need the platform to take care of its maintenance and the software support that goes with it. In other words, the system needs to handle APAF’s upkeep and software support rather than relying on another service. Can we confirm that's included?

- Helen mentioned the APAF Operations Procedures Document must detail installation-dependent requirements and provide comprehensive instructions to eliminate the need for additional user training.

- Ian noted the importance of calibrating and validating data before submission to the NASA Planetary Data System, with a maximum turnaround of six months.

- Emily reminded the team about the need for built-in error handling mechanisms to ensure system reliability.

- Grace requested clarification on how password protection will be implemented on the web server and whether this will affect performance.

- Follow-up point: To speed up the end-of-line calibration, the team wants to jog the gantry with the safety gate open so they can tweak the camera alignment. They're okay bypassing the door interlock for up to 10 minutes, as long as the motion is capped at 0.5 m/s, and they don't plan to set up extra guarding for this. QA said they'll post a spotter, but otherwise keep the procedure as-is.

- Raised during discussion: Given the operator feedback, we want to keep the behavior simple. There shouldn't be multiple modes; it should always run in a single operating state with no mode switching. That will make support and training easier.

- Side note from a stakeholder: Legal came back pretty firm on this: we’re not going to package or redistribute the SwRI IDFS access and science analysis tools to the Co-Is. Instead, they’ll work through the ESA-hosted web viewer for their needs, and the SwRI binaries will remain on the operations side only. This keeps us clear of the licensing and support overhead.

- Frank responded that password protection will be integrated at the web server level, using standard authentication protocols, designed to minimize performance impact.

- Helen proposed that the system's software design document should fully define all data products within each virtual instrument, including instrument and spacecraft parameters.

- Emily stressed that all requirements must be objectively verifiable, concise, and feasible within the project schedule and budget.

- Ian suggested simulating instrument data sets from calibration data to test processing algorithms and validate outputs using the Southwest Data Display and Analysis System.

Action Items:

1. Frank to draft the initial system architecture document, including UNIX environment specifications and archival integration. (Due: April 5, 2025)
2. Grace to develop a security plan detailing password protection mechanisms for web displays and servers. (Due: April 10, 2025)
3. Helen to begin drafting the APAF Operations Procedures Document with focus on installation and operational instructions. (Due: April 15, 2025)
4. Ian to prepare a plan for data calibration, validation, and simulation testing using SDDAS. (Due: April 20, 2025)
5. Emily to coordinate with Co-Investigators to define data distribution requirements and timelines. (Due: April 12, 2025)

Decisions Made:

- System will operate in a single mode to simplify control.
- Password protection is mandatory for all web-based data access until public release.
- UNIX-based development and production systems with archival capabilities are confirmed.
- Data distribution to Co-Investigators within 24 hours post-acquisition is required.

Open Questions:

- What specific authentication protocols will be used for web server password protection?
- How will the system handle error detection and recovery during telemetry acquisition and processing?
- Are there any installation-dependent requirements that might impact deployment timelines?

Next Meeting: April 22, 2025, to review draft documents and progress on action items.

source_texts.title: Clarifications and Confirmations on ASPERA-3 Data System Requirements
source_texts.text:
Email 1:
From: Mark (SwRI Software Engineer)
To: Lisa (ASPERA-3 Project Manager)
Subject: Clarification on Data Distribution Timing and Formats

Hi Lisa,

I wanted to confirm the expectations around data distribution to Co-Investigators. The brief mentions distribution within 24 hours of telemetry acquisition, but are there any contingencies if processing delays occur? Also, should the system distribute raw telemetry, cleaned-up intermediate files, or only the final IDFS data sets?

Thanks,
Mark

---

Email 2:
From: Lisa (ASPERA-3 Project Manager)
To: Mark (SwRI Software Engineer)
Subject: RE: Clarification on Data Distribution Timing and Formats

Hi Mark,

Good questions. The intent is to distribute all relevant data electronically within 24 hours, including ASPERA-3 and Mars Express Orbit/Attitude IDFS data plus any intermediate cleaned-up telemetry files generated by APAF if ESOC does not provide them. This is contingent on error-free transmission and processing. If errors occur, distribution may be delayed until resolved.

On another note, Following up on yesterday’s call, to reduce churn we’ll keep the ASPERA-3 MO analysis tool PI-only for the first 60 days after each build lands in the NASA repo. The broader science community will only get access once the embargo lifts, with downloads opening on day 61. This staggered rollout gives the PI team time to validate outputs before we open it up more widely.

Let me know if you need more details.

Best,
Lisa

---

Email 3:
From: Mark (SwRI Software Engineer)
To: Lisa (ASPERA-3 Project Manager), Tom (Security Officer)
Subject: Questions About Web Access Security and Password Protection

Hi Lisa and Tom,

Regarding the web-based data displays, can you clarify the password protection requirements? Should all displays be password protected until public release, or only certain ones? Also, what about the web server itself—does that require access restrictions?

Thanks,
Mark

---

Email 4:
From: Tom (Security Officer)
To: Mark (SwRI Software Engineer), Lisa (ASPERA-3 Project Manager)
Subject: RE: Questions About Web Access Security and Password Protection

Hi Mark,

All web-based displays defined by the ASPERA-3 team for science analysis must be password protected until the data is made public. Additionally, the web server hosting these displays must have appropriate password protection to restrict access to authorized ASPERA-3 team members only.

This is to ensure data security and controlled access during the proprietary period.

Regards,
Tom

---

Quick follow-up: In reviewing the latest list, a few items still read like vague wishes. For the next revision, make sure every requirement spells out the acceptance condition so we can verify it objectively, keep it to one simple, concise idea, and avoid bundling. Also sanity-check that each one is technically doable with our stack and realistically fits the current timeline and budget.

One more thing — Quick reminder for the ops plan: after each telemetry pass comes in, the system needs to electronically push the ASPERA-3 data together with the Mars Express orbit/attitude IDFS and any APAF-produced intermediate cleaned telemetry files to all ASPERA-3 co‑investigators. The turnaround should be within 24 hours of receipt, but only if the transmission and processing finish without any errors. That way the whole Co‑I team has the same package by the next day.

Email 5:
From: Mark (SwRI Software Engineer)
To: Lisa (ASPERA-3 Project Manager), Tom (Security Officer)
Subject: Confirmation of System Constraints and Safety Requirements

Hi Lisa and Tom,

One last point—are there any constraints on system operation modes or safety requirements we should be aware of? For example, should the system operate in multiple modes or just one? Also, any safety considerations to prevent hazards?

Thanks,
Mark

---

Email 6:
From: Lisa (ASPERA-3 Project Manager)
To: Mark (SwRI Software Engineer), Tom (Security Officer)
Subject: RE: Confirmation of System Constraints and Safety Requirements

Hi Mark,

The system is required to operate in only one mode to simplify control and reduce complexity. Regarding safety, the system must not impose any hazards to personnel, property, or the environment. These constraints are critical and must be incorporated into the design.

Please let me know if you need further clarification.

Best,
Lisa

gold_requirements.text: The system shall process and archive data for the Mars Express mission as part of the ASPERA-3 Processing and Archiving Facility (APAF) ground data system.
The APAF data system shall acquire ASPERA-3 telemetry data via NISN and from ESOC, including Mars Express Orbit/Attitude telemetry.
gold_requirements.source_region: 1.2 Data System Overview, 3.1 Capability or Functional Requirements

gold_requirements.text: The APAF data system shall process telemetry data into Instrument Data File Set (IDFS) data sets, including all ASPERA-3 science data and engineering and ancillary information necessary for calibration and science validation.
gold_requirements.source_region: 1.2 Data System Overview, 3.1 Capability or Functional Requirements, raw_chunk_0001, raw_chunk_0002

gold_requirements.text: The APAF data system shall define IDFS data sets based on the physical instruments and ancillary data necessary for scientific analysis and divide each physical component of ASPERA-3 into logical groups called virtual instruments, each formatted as an IDFS data set.
The APAF data system shall generate intermediate files of cleaned-up ASPERA-3 and Mars Express Orbit/Attitude telemetry if cleaned-up telemetry is not provided by ESOC.
The APAF data system shall store telemetry, IDFS data sets, and any APAF-generated intermediate files of cleaned-up telemetry on a local Southwest Research Institute (SwRI) archive.
gold_requirements.source_region: 1.2 Data System Overview, APAF PDMP, raw_chunk_0002

gold_requirements.text: The APAF data system shall submit ASPERA-3 IDFS data sets to the Planetary Data System (PDS) for long-term archival in PDS-compliant form, after calibration and validation, no later than six months after acquisition.

The system shall distribute ASPERA-3 and Mars Express Orbit/Attitude IDFS data and any APAF-generated intermediate files of cleaned-up telemetry electronically to all ASPERA-3 Co-Investigators within 24 hours of acquiring telemetry, provided transmission and processing are error-free.

The system shall provide web-based displays of the most current ASPERA-3 data for public viewing, with password protection until data is made public, and displays defined by the ASPERA-3 team that allow use of any available ASPERA-3 data for science analysis.
gold_requirements.source_region: 1.2 Data System Overview, APAF PDMP, [Page 10], raw_chunk_0001, raw_chunk_0002

gold_requirements.text: The system shall password protect web-based displays defined by the ASPERA-3 team for science analysis until the ASPERA-3 data is made public.
The APAF system shall have built-in error handling to ensure reliability.

The system shall provide ASPERA-3 and Mars Express Orbit/Attitude IDFS data and any APAF-generated intermediate files of cleaned-up telemetry to all ASPERA-3 Co-Investigators and make IDFS data access software and science analysis software developed by SwRI available to them.
The system shall use the www.ASPERA-3.org website for obtaining and reviewing IDFS information.
The APAF system web server shall be password protected where appropriate to allow only pertinent ASPERA-3 team members access.
The system shall provide a UNIX-based APAF system development computer, a UNIX-based APAF system production computer with an archival system, and a web server for the APAF system.
The system shall provide a network connection between SwRI and NISN for data transfers and distribution.
The system shall provide APAF system maintenance and software support.
The system shall calibrate and validate ASPERA-3 data prior to depositing it in NASA PDS.

The system shall determine the exact ASPERA-3 and Mars Express Orbit/Attitude data sets to be distributed to each Co-Investigator based on needs and available resources six months prior to Mars Express launch and clearly define and describe the distribution mechanisms in the APAF Operation Procedures Document.
The system shall document any identified installation-dependent requirements in the APAF Operations Procedures Document.

The APAF Operations Procedures Document shall provide installation and operations instructions detailed enough to eliminate training-related requirements for users and operators of the APAF data system.

The system shall have an associated Software Design Document (SDD) that fully defines and describes all data products contained within each individual virtual instrument, including instrument parameters, spacecraft parameters, science information, internal interfaces, and virtual instrument data items.
gold_requirements.source_region: [Page 7], [Page 11], 3.3 Internal Interface Requirements, 3.4 Internal Data Requirements

gold_requirements.text: The system shall ensure each requirement states a condition for system acceptance, is stated to allow objective verification, is singular, concise, simple, and technically feasible and achievable within schedule and budget.
The system shall assign a project-unique identifier to each requirement and annotate each requirement with associated qualification (verification) methods, rationale, and traceability to its source.

The system shall verify each requirement using annotated qualification methods and support verification by demonstration without special equipment and by analysis using accumulated data from characterization, calibration, or testing.

The system shall create simulated instrument data sets from accumulated calibration and test data formatted as expected telemetry input, process these into IDFS data sets, and analyze the output using the Southwest Data Display and Analysis System (SDDAS).

The system shall document the relationship between each requirement and the project item(s) it addresses, supporting requirements addressing multiple project items and higher-level design elements, and provide for each requirement: Identifier, Description, Source, and Verification Method.
The ASPERA-3 Mission of Opportunity shall provide the complete data set of the integrated ASPERA-3 investigation to all ASPERA-3 Co-Investigators and the NASA Planetary Data System.
The ASPERA-3 Mission of Opportunity shall deliver specific measurements in the energy range of 0.1 to 100 keV.

SwRI shall develop the ASPERA-3 data reduction system and software for instrument integration, test, calibration, and mission operations of the full ASPERA-3 experiment and interface with all ASPERA-3 sensor teams to determine and effect data packetization and storage strategies.
gold_requirements.source_region: 4.1.1.5 Measure ions and electrons in the energy range of 0.001 to 40 keV; 1-1e6 amu/q.

gold_requirements.text: The data processing software shall meet the delivery requirements of the IRF to support the launch date and operation of the ESA Mars Express mission.
gold_requirements.source_region: 4.1.1.5 Measure ions and electrons in the energy range of 0.001 to 40 keV; 1-1e6 amu/q.

gold_requirements.text: SwRI shall provide processing algorithms to IRF in preparation for mission operations and data analysis of Mars Express on a schedule that satisfies mission needs per agreement with IRF.
gold_requirements.source_region: 4.1.1.5 Measure ions and electrons in the energy range of 0.001 to 40 keV; 1-1e6 amu/q.

gold_requirements.text: The ASPERA-3 MO PI shall collect engineering and ancillary information necessary to validate and calibrate scientific data before depositing it in the NASA PDS in a PDS-compliant form.
gold_requirements.source_region: 4.1.1.5 Measure ions and electrons in the energy range of 0.001 to 40 keV; 1-1e6 amu/q.

gold_requirements.text: The time to complete validation and calibration of scientific data shall be the minimum necessary to provide accurate data but shall not exceed six months after acquisition.
gold_requirements.source_region: 4.1.1.5 Measure ions and electrons in the energy range of 0.001 to 40 keV; 1-1e6 amu/q.

gold_requirements.text: The system shall integrate science analysis software developed by the ASPERA-3 MO PI team into the NASA approved data repository and provide equal online access to the science analysis software for the PI team and the science community.

The ASPERA-3 MO Project shall develop a project data management plan addressing planning, acquisition, processing, data product generation and validation, archiving, and preservation of science data, identifying science and supplementary data products, associated handling systems, and defining roles, responsibilities, and operational interfaces affecting those data and systems.
The system shall operate in only one state or mode.
The system shall not impose hazards to personnel, property, or the environment.

original_req_texts: The system shall verify each requirement using annotated qualification methods and support verification by demonstration without special equipment and by analysis using accumulated data from characterization, calibration, or testing.
seed_registry.injected_text: From our side, the verification demos have to be executed on the lab’s HIL bench using the proprietary DAQ and the calibrated vibration table. We won’t accept a field demonstration without that special equipment, because QA needs the bench traces for sign-off.
seed_registry.defect_description: The interview text requires demonstrations to use special equipment (HIL bench, proprietary DAQ, vibration table), which conflicts with the gold requirement that demonstrations be supported without special equipment.

original_req_texts: The system shall provide ASPERA-3 and Mars Express Orbit/Attitude IDFS data and any APAF-generated intermediate files of cleaned-up telemetry to all ASPERA-3 Co-Investigators and make IDFS data access software and science analysis software developed by SwRI available to them.
seed_registry.injected_text: Legal came back pretty firm on this: we’re not going to package or redistribute the SwRI IDFS access and science analysis tools to the Co-Is. Instead, they’ll work through the ESA-hosted web viewer for their needs, and the SwRI binaries will remain on the operations side only. This keeps us clear of the licensing and support overhead.
seed_registry.defect_description: The gold requirement says the SwRI-developed IDFS data access and science analysis software must be made available to all ASPERA-3 Co-Investigators, but the injected text states those SwRI tools will not be redistributed to Co-Is and will remain internal.

original_req_texts: The system shall integrate science analysis software developed by the ASPERA-3 MO PI team into the NASA approved data repository and provide equal online access to the science analysis software for the PI team and the science community.
seed_registry.injected_text: Following up on yesterday’s call, to reduce churn we’ll keep the ASPERA-3 MO analysis tool PI-only for the first 60 days after each build lands in the NASA repo. The broader science community will only get access once the embargo lifts, with downloads opening on day 61. This staggered rollout gives the PI team time to validate outputs before we open it up more widely.
seed_registry.defect_description: Proposes a 60-day PI-only embargo before community access, which conflicts with the requirement to provide equal online access to the PI team and the science community.

original_req_texts: The APAF system shall have built-in error handling to ensure reliability.
seed_registry.injected_text: For the first rollout, we'd like APAF to skip any local error handling and just pass exceptions straight up to the orchestrator unchanged. No internal retries, no circuit breakers, and please don't wrap or mask errors—the gateway's global handler will take care of all that. This keeps failure behavior consistent across services.
seed_registry.defect_description: The interview text requires APAF to have zero local error handling (no retries, circuit breakers, or error wrapping) and to forward errors upstream, which conflicts with the gold requirement that APAF include built-in error handling to ensure reliability.

original_req_texts: The system shall not impose hazards to personnel, property, or the environment.
seed_registry.injected_text: To speed up the end-of-line calibration, the team wants to jog the gantry with the safety gate open so they can tweak the camera alignment. They're okay bypassing the door interlock for up to 10 minutes, as long as the motion is capped at 0.5 m/s, and they don't plan to set up extra guarding for this. QA said they'll post a spotter, but otherwise keep the procedure as-is.
seed_registry.defect_description: Allowing the gantry to move with the safety gate open by bypassing the interlock for up to 10 minutes at 0.5 m/s (without added guarding) introduces a personnel hazard, contradicting the requirement that the system not impose hazards to personnel, property, or the environment.

original_req_texts: The system shall operate in only one state or mode.
seed_registry.injected_text: Given the operator feedback, we want to keep the behavior simple. There shouldn't be multiple modes; it should always run in a single operating state with no mode switching. That will make support and training easier.
seed_registry.defect_description: This paraphrases and duplicates PURE_2001 - esa_R037 by stating the system operates in only one state or mode.

original_req_texts: The system shall ensure each requirement states a condition for system acceptance, is stated to allow objective verification, is singular, concise, simple, and technically feasible and achievable within schedule and budget.
seed_registry.injected_text: In reviewing the latest list, a few items still read like vague wishes. For the next revision, make sure every requirement spells out the acceptance condition so we can verify it objectively, keep it to one simple, concise idea, and avoid bundling. Also sanity-check that each one is technically doable with our stack and realistically fits the current timeline and budget.
seed_registry.defect_description: This restates the same requirement as PURE_2001 - esa_R023 about each requirement having explicit acceptance criteria, being objectively verifiable, singular, concise, and feasible within the schedule and budget.

original_req_texts: The system shall not impose hazards to personnel, property, or the environment.
seed_registry.injected_text: Before we get into features, I want to stress safety. The system must not introduce any hazards to our staff, to facilities or equipment, or to the environment. If something could put people at risk, damage property, or harm the environment, we need to rethink it.
seed_registry.defect_description: This paraphrase duplicates requirement PURE_2001 - esa_R038 by restating that the system must not pose hazards to people, property, or the environment.

original_req_texts: The system shall provide APAF system maintenance and software support.
seed_registry.injected_text: Quick check on APAF: we need the platform to take care of its maintenance and the software support that goes with it. In other words, the system needs to handle APAF’s upkeep and software support rather than relying on another service. Can we confirm that's included?
seed_registry.defect_description: This restates the same need as PURE_2001 - esa_R017, namely that the system provides APAF system maintenance and software support.

original_req_texts: The system shall distribute ASPERA-3 and Mars Express Orbit/Attitude IDFS data and any APAF-generated intermediate files of cleaned-up telemetry electronically to all ASPERA-3 Co-Investigators within 24 hours of acquiring telemetry, provided transmission and processing are error-free.
seed_registry.injected_text: Quick reminder for the ops plan: after each telemetry pass comes in, the system needs to electronically push the ASPERA-3 data together with the Mars Express orbit/attitude IDFS and any APAF-produced intermediate cleaned telemetry files to all ASPERA-3 co‑investigators. The turnaround should be within 24 hours of receipt, but only if the transmission and processing finish without any errors. That way the whole Co‑I team has the same package by the next day.
seed_registry.defect_description: This restates the same requirement as PURE_2001 - esa_R008 in different words.

brief.domain: space science data processing and archiving
business_goals: Provide timely and validated scientific data products for Mars Express mission
Ensure long-term archival and public accessibility of ASPERA-3 data
Support science team with data processing, calibration, and analysis tools
Maintain compliance with NASA PDS standards and mission schedules
core_features: Acquisition of telemetry data from NISN and ESOC including spacecraft orbit and attitude
Processing raw telemetry into standardized Instrument Data File Sets (IDFS)
Division of instrument data into logical virtual instruments for analysis
Distribution of data and analysis software to Co-Investigators within 24 hours
Web-based data visualization with controlled access until public release
Archival of validated data in PDS within six months of receipt
Provision of error handling and maintenance support for system reliability
quality_concerns: Data security through password protection of web displays and servers
System reliability with built-in error handling mechanisms
Objective and verifiable requirements ensuring feasibility and schedule adherence
Minimizing validation and calibration time without compromising data accuracy
constraints: Use of UNIX-based development and production environments with archival capabilities
Operation restricted to a single system mode to simplify control
Compliance with safety requirements to avoid hazards to personnel and environment
Comprehensive Software Design Document detailing data products and interfaces
terminology: ASPERA-3: Instrument package on Mars Express measuring particles and fields
IDFS: Instrument Data File Set formatting scientific and ancillary data
Virtual Instrument: Logical grouping of instrument components within IDFS
NISN: NASA Integrated Services Network for telemetry data transfer
PDS: NASA Planetary Data System for long-term data archiving
ESOC: European Science Operations Center providing telemetry data
APAF: ASPERA-3 Processing and Archiving Facility ground data system
